description: Almost impossible to kill! Mint is incredibly vigorous and perfect for beginners. Keep it in its own pot — it spreads aggressively and will take over anything it's planted with. Great for tea, cocktails, and cooking.

characteristics.sunRequirement: Partial shade to full sun (3+ hours)
characteristics.waterNeeds: high and THEN info: Regular — likes moist soil

pinchOutTops.info: Not typically required for this plant.

care.plantOut: Buy a mint plant (supermarket ones work fine!) and repot into a 5L+ container. IMPORTANT: Always keep mint in its own pot — it will strangle other plants.
care.watering: Keep moist. Mint is one of the few herbs that likes damp soil. Water daily in hot weather.
care.feeding: Feed monthly with a balanced liquid fertiliser during summer.
care.harvest: Pick leaves and stems as needed. Regular harvesting keeps the plant bushy and productive. Cut back hard in midsummer if it gets leggy.
care.overwintering: Leave outdoors — fully hardy. It dies back in winter and bounces back in spring. Repot every 2 years in fresh compost.

propagation.seedDescription: Mint can be grown from seed but it's unreliable — seedlings are often weak and variable. Buy plants instead.
propagation.cuttingsMethod: Cut a 10cm stem, strip lower leaves, and place in water. Roots appear in about a week. Plant into compost. Ridiculously easy.
propagation.divisionMethod: In spring, tip out the root ball, cut into sections with a sharp knife, and repot each piece. Each section will grow into a new plant.

deadheading.notes: Not essential. Cutting back before or during flowering encourages fresh leafy growth. The flowers attract bees, so leaving some is beneficial.

seedSaving.seedPodDescription: Mint rarely sets viable seed. Propagate by cuttings or division instead.

commonProblems: Mint rust — orange/brown spots on leaves. Remove and destroy affected stems. Don't compost them.
Spreading too aggressively — ALWAYS keep in its own pot. Never plant with other herbs.
Leggy growth — cut back hard in midsummer for a flush of fresh leaves.
funFact: In Greek mythology, mint was originally a nymph called Minthe who was transformed into a plant by the jealous goddess Persephone. But she retained her wonderful fragrance.
